content.problemStatement:
Harasees has an array AAA of size NNN containing non-negative integers. He starts at the index 111 of AAA and wants to reach the index NNN. In one move, he can do the following:


If he is currently at index iii, then he can move to any index jjj such that i≤j≤min⁡(i+Ai,N)i \le j \le \min(i + A_i, N)i≤j≤min(i+Ai​,N).
The cost of a path is defined as the bitwise OR of all the array elements visited by Harasees during his journey from index 111 to NNN. Formally if he visited the indices 1,X1,X2,…,XK,N1, X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_K, N1,X1​,X2​,…,XK​,N, then the cost of this path =(A1 ∣ AX1 ∣ AX2 ∣ … ∣ AXK ∣ AN) = (A_1 \ | \ A_{X_1} \ | \ A_{X_2} \ | \ \ldots \ | \ A_{X_K} \ | \ A_N)=(A1​ ∣ AX1​​ ∣ AX2​​ ∣ … ∣ AXK​​ ∣ AN​).

Determine the **minimum** possible cost to reach index NNN from index 111. If it is not possible to do so, output −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the length of the array AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output the **minimum** cost to go from index 111 to index NNN using the given moves. If it is not possible to do so, output −1-1−1.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: Harasees has no choice but to keep going from his current position to the position that follows it immediately till he reaches the 555th index. So the minimum cost (in this case, the only possible cost) is 1 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 = 11\ |\ 1\ |\ 1\ |\ 1\ |\ 1\ =\ 11 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 ∣ 1 = 1.
Test Case 2: It is optimal for Harasees to make the following moves: (i=1)→(i=3)→(i=4)(i = 1) \rightarrow (i = 3) \rightarrow (i = 4)(i=1)→(i=3)→(i=4). The cost of this path is: A1 ∣ A3 ∣ A4=2 ∣ 1 ∣ 3 = 3A_1 \ | \ A_3 \ | \ A_4 = 2\ |\ 1\ |\ 3\ =\ 3A1​ ∣ A3​ ∣ A4​=2 ∣ 1 ∣ 3 = 3. We can prove that no path exists with a cost <3\lt 3<3.

Explanation:
Test Case 1: In this case Harasees will get stuck at index 222 and there is no way to reach the last index.
